topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a cold, sharp intrusion into the warm glow of my laptop. My breath hitched. I clicked, and the headline swam into view: "The Luminary's Insight on Modern Art." My stomach plummeted. I scrolled down, heart hammering against my ribs like a trapped bird. The article was mine—my carefully crafted analysis, my unique perspective—but the byline read 'Elias Vance.' Elias Vance. The name was a phantom touch, a ghost in my own work. A slow, burning heat spread through my chest, making my hands tremble as I reread the text, searching for any trace of my voice, any signature that belonged to me. It wasn't there. A strange, dizzying sensation washed over me, a dizzying sense of being utterly misplaced. I leaned back in my chair, staring blankly at the ceiling, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears.

[story 2]

I found the email tucked away in a forgotten folder, the subject line a bland corporate memo, but the contents were a wrecking ball to my composure. My piece, the one I’d poured weeks into, was credited to someone else. The realization hit me with the force of a physical blow. I felt a flush creep up my neck, hot and undeniable. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my apartment, the floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. Every instinct screamed at me to confront the editor, to demand answers, but a strange paralysis held me captive. My gaze kept drifting towards the window, where the city lights blurred into streaks of color. A breathless quality tightened my throat. It felt like a secret, illicit knowledge, and the sheer audacity of the situation made my palms slick with sudden perspiration.

[story 3]

When the plagiarism checker flagged the discrepancy, the world seemed to tilt. I stared at the screen, the words mocking me from the page. My jaw tightened, a muscle jumping beneath my skin. I traced the borrowed signature with a fingertip, the ink feeling alien and wrong. A wave of intense yearning washed over me, so potent it made my vision swim slightly. I wanted to reach out, to pull the narrative back, to reclaim the space where my ideas resided. A low, involuntary sound escaped my lips—a soft, almost wounded sigh. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to rewrite everything, to reshape the very landscape of my career around the phantom author, a desperate, consuming desire to be seen through those eyes.
